title: Subcontractor Access — Downstream API Keys Touching ePHI Interfaces

compliance.assurance_rationale: HIPAA Security §164.502(e) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that HIPAA Security §164.502(e) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.10.53: Subcontractor Access — Downstream API Keys Touching ePHI Interfaces.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.10.53' running on sourcetype apigee:proxy and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=interop` `sourcetype=apigee:proxy` or AWS API Gateway logs (`client_id`, `api_key`, `consumer`), `lookup ba_subcontractor_clients.csv`
app: HTTP Event Collector (HEC), Splunk Enterprise Security (263)
spl:
index=interop sourcetype="apigee:proxy" uri_path="/fhir/*" earliest=-30d
| stats earliest(_time) as first_seen latest(_time) as last_seen dc(status) as status_codes by client_id, api_key
| eval key_or_client=coalesce(client_id, api_key)
| lookup ba_subcontractor_clients.csv key_or_client OUTPUT approved_vendor, subcontractor_name
| where isnull(approved_vendor)
| sort first_seen
description: Monitors first-seen API keys or OAuth clients used by BA subcontractors against an approved key registry so the extended BAA chain does not introduce unreviewed ePHI paths.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for HIPAA Security compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes interop and sourcetypes apigee:proxy, apigee:proxy`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Hash static API keys at ingest with `sha256(api_key)` for display fields; (2) require procurement to register subcontractor keys before production; (3) auto-revoke via API management integration on critical alerts. Indexes required: `index=interop`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=apigee:proxy`, `sourcetype=apigee:proxy``; Lookups: `ba_subcontractor_clients`, `ba_subcontractor_clients.csv`.
visualization: Table (first_seen, key hash, path), Timeline (new clients per week).
knownFalsePositives: PHI access from authorized clinical workflows, billing operations, audit reviews, or BAA partners.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: HTTP Event Collector (HEC), Splunk Enterprise Security (263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=interop` `sourcetype=apigee:proxy` or AWS API Gateway logs (`client_id`, `api_key`, `consumer`), `lookup ba_subcontractor_clients.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Hash static API keys at ingest with `sha256(api_key)` for display fields; (2) require procurement to register subcontractor keys before production; (3) auto-revoke via API management integration on critical alerts.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=interop sourcetype="apigee:proxy" uri_path="/fhir/*" earliest=-30d
| stats earliest(_time) as first_seen latest(_time) as last_seen dc(status) as status_codes by client_id, api_key
| eval key_or_client=coalesce(client_id, api_key)
| lookup ba_subcontractor_clients.csv key_or_client OUTPUT approved_vendor, subcontractor_name
| where isnull(approved_vendor)
| sort first_seen
```

Understanding this SPL

**Subcontractor Access — Downstream API Keys Touching ePHI Interfaces (§164.502(e) / BAA chain)** — Monitors first-seen API keys or OAuth clients used by BA subcontractors against an approved key registry so the extended BAA chain does not introduce unreviewed ePHI paths.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=interop` `sourcetype=apigee:proxy` or AWS API Gateway logs (`client_id`, `api_key`, `consumer`), `lookup ba_subcontractor_clients.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): HTTP Event Collector (HEC), Splunk Enterprise Security (263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: interop; **sourcetype**: apigee:proxy. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=interop, sourcetype="apigee:proxy", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by client_id, api_key** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **key_or_client** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnull(approved_vendor)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the EHR audit trail (Epic, Cerner), walk known-good cases from the same workflow class, and confirm index coverage, field extractions, and access permissions match expectations.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (first_seen, key hash, path), Timeline (new clients per week).
grandmaExplanation: We monitor first-seen API keys or OAuth clients used by BA subcontractors against an approved key registry so the extended BAA chain does not introduce unreviewed ePHI paths. HIPAA is about keeping health records private and secure when we use them in care and operations.
